title: Electrical power and energy — what it costs to run your devices
examTip: Multi-step calculation questions are common — write down what you know, pick the right equation, substitute carefully, and always include units. Practice is the only way to get fast at this.
introTips: Before you start: can you write down P = IV, E = Pt and V = IR from memory? If not, that's your first job.
Try estimating the power of five appliances at home before reading — then check how close you were.
Pay attention to unit conversions: watts ↔ kilowatts, seconds ↔ hours. These trip people up in exams.
intro: Every time you boil a kettle, stream a video or charge your phone, you are paying for electrical energy — and the bill at the end of the month is a direct consequence of the physics you are about to learn. Understanding power and energy is not just abstract maths: it tells you which appliances cost the most to run, why LED bulbs save money, and how to work out the exact cost of a tumble dryer cycle. These equations also come up in almost every electricity exam question.

keyPoints.heading: Electrical power
keyPoints.content: Power is the rate at which energy is transferred — how many joules per second. The unit of power is the watt (W), where 1 W = 1 J/s. The key equations are: P = IV (power = current × voltage), P = I²R (obtained by substituting V = IR into P = IV), and P = V²/R (obtained by substituting I = V/R). Worked example 1: a kettle with I = 10 A and V = 230 V → P = 10 × 230 = 2300 W = 2.3 kW. Worked example 2: a phone charger with I = 1 A, V = 5 V → P = 1 × 5 = 5 W. Worked example 3: a resistor with R = 20 Ω and I = 3 A → P = 3² × 20 = 9 × 20 = 180 W. Typical values to know: kettle ~2 kW, microwave ~800 W, laptop ~65 W, LED bulb ~10 W, old incandescent bulb ~60 W. 1 kW = 1000 W.
writeBullets: Power — rate of energy transfer; measured in watts (W); 1 W = 1 J/s
P = IV (primary equation: power = current × voltage)
P = I²R (substitute V = IR into P = IV)
P = V²/R (substitute I = V/R into P = IV)
Kettle: I = 10 A, V = 230 V → P = 2300 W = 2.3 kW
1 kW = 1000 W; typical appliances: kettle 2 kW, LED bulb 10 W, laptop 65 W

keyTerms.def: The rate of energy transfer; measured in watts (W). P = IV.
The unit of power; 1 W = 1 joule per second.
1000 watts; used for higher-power appliances like kettles and heaters.

keyPoints.memTip: PIV — Power = I × V. Think of it as 'PIV-ot' — the central equation everything else turns around. P = I²R and P = V²/R are just substitutions you can always derive if you forget them.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often use P = IV but forget they can also use P = I²R when voltage is not given. Show them how all three equations are the same equation in different forms — no new physics, just algebra.

keyPoints.heading: Electrical energy
keyPoints.content: Energy transferred = power × time: E = Pt. Units: joules (J) when P is in watts (W) and t is in seconds (s). Substituting P = IV gives E = IVt. Worked example 1: a 2 kW kettle running for 3 minutes → E = 2000 × (3 × 60) = 2000 × 180 = 360,000 J = 360 kJ. Worked example 2: a 60 W bulb left on for 1 hour → E = 60 × 3600 = 216,000 J = 216 kJ. For billing purposes, electricity companies use a much larger unit: the kilowatt-hour (kWh). 1 kWh = energy transferred by a 1 kW device running for 1 hour = 1000 W × 3600 s = 3,600,000 J = 3.6 MJ. Units used (kWh) = Power (kW) × time (hours). Worked example 3: a 2 kW kettle for 30 minutes = 2 kW × 0.5 h = 1 kWh.
writeBullets: E = Pt (energy = power × time); units: joules when P in W and t in seconds
E = IVt (substitute P = IV)
Always convert time to seconds before using joules
kWh: energy unit for billing; 1 kWh = energy from 1 kW device for 1 hour
Units used (kWh) = Power (kW) × time (hours)

keyTerms.def: The total energy moved from one store to another; E = Pt (joules) or Power (kW) × time (hours) for kWh.
The unit electricity companies use for billing; the energy transferred by a 1 kW device in 1 hour = 3.6 MJ.
The SI unit of energy; 1 J = 1 W × 1 s.

keyPoints.memTip: Joules = watts × seconds (small scale). kWh = kilowatts × hours (big scale for bills). The physics is identical — just different units for different situations.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Ensure students know when to use seconds (for joules) and when to use hours (for kWh). Mixing them is the most common error in energy calculations.

keyPoints.heading: Cost of electricity
keyPoints.content: Once you know how many kilowatt-hours an appliance uses, you can calculate the cost. Cost = units used (kWh) × price per unit (p/kWh). The price per kWh is given in the exam question — a typical value is around 28p per kWh (this varies; always use the figure given). Worked example 1: a 2.5 kW tumble dryer runs for 2 hours → units = 2.5 × 2 = 5 kWh → cost = 5 × 28p = 140p = £1.40. Worked example 2: a 100 W television watched for 4 hours → units = 0.1 kW × 4 h = 0.4 kWh → cost = 0.4 × 28p = 11.2p. Energy saving strategies: switch to LED bulbs (10 W vs 60 W incandescent for the same light output), turn devices off standby, take shorter showers (if using electric showers), use appliances at off-peak times. Payback time = extra cost of efficient appliance ÷ annual saving. Example: energy-efficient boiler costs £800 more but saves £200/year → payback time = 800 ÷ 200 = 4 years. After 4 years it starts saving money.
writeBullets: Cost (pence) = units (kWh) × price per kWh (p)
Units used (kWh) = power (kW) × time (hours) — convert watts to kilowatts first
Worked example: 2.5 kW dryer × 2 h = 5 kWh × 28p = £1.40
Payback time = extra upfront cost ÷ annual saving (in years)
LED bulbs: ~10 W vs 60 W incandescent — same light, 83% less energy
Price per kWh is always given in the exam — do not memorise a fixed value

keyTerms.def: Cost (p) = units used (kWh) × price per unit (p/kWh). Units = power (kW) × time (hours).
The time it takes for savings from an energy-efficient appliance to recoup the extra purchase cost. Payback time = extra cost ÷ annual saving.
Using less energy to achieve the same output; e.g. LED bulbs give the same light as incandescent bulbs for a fraction of the power.

keyPoints.memTip: Units → Cost: Power (kW) × hours = kWh. kWh × pence = pence. Always do it in that order and you won't go wrong.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students sometimes forget to convert watts to kilowatts before calculating kWh — they get answers 1000× too large. Build the habit of writing 'P = ___ W = ___ kW' before every calculation.

keyPoints.heading: Linking all the electrical equations
keyPoints.content: AQA requires you to select and combine equations across a multi-step calculation. Here is how everything links together: V = IR (Ohm's Law: voltage = current × resistance). P = IV (power). Substituting: P = I²R and P = V²/R. E = Pt (energy = power × time). Substituting: E = IVt. Q = It (charge = current × time; Q in coulombs, C). E = QV (energy = charge × voltage — this links the mechanical 'work done' definition of energy to the circuit quantities). Multi-step strategy: (1) write down all the values you know; (2) identify what the question asks for; (3) select the equation that connects what you know to what you need; (4) substitute values; (5) rearrange if needed; (6) check units. Worked multi-step example: a resistor with R = 50 Ω and V = 10 V, find energy transferred in 2 minutes. Step 1: I = V/R = 10/50 = 0.2 A. Step 2: P = IV = 0.2 × 10 = 2 W. Step 3: E = Pt = 2 × 120 = 240 J.
writeBullets: V = IR — Ohm's Law; links voltage, current, resistance
P = IV — power; leads to P = I²R and P = V²/R by substitution
E = Pt = IVt — energy transferred in joules
Q = It — charge (coulombs) = current × time
E = QV — energy = charge × voltage; links work done to circuit quantities
Multi-step strategy: list knowns → pick equation → substitute → rearrange → check units

keyTerms.def: The quantity of electricity flowing past a point; Q = It. Unit: coulombs (C).
Energy transferred (J) = charge (C) × voltage (V). Connects the electrical quantities to the concept of work done.
A calculation requiring two or more equations applied in sequence; common in AQA electricity questions.

keyPoints.memTip: Draw a spider diagram with P in the middle and all four equations radiating out: P=IV, P=I²R, P=V²/R, E=Pt. Then link V=IR as the bridge between them. Every electricity calculation lives on that diagram.
keyPoints.tutor_note: E = QV is often neglected but appears in higher-grade questions. It's really just energy = work done to move charge Q through a potential difference V — the same concept as W = Fd but for electrical charge.

rows: V in V, I in A, R in Ω
Finding voltage, current or resistance when two of the three are known
P in W, I in A, V in V
P in W, I in A, R in Ω
P in W, V in V, R in Ω
E in J, P in W, t in s
Finding energy transferred; convert t to seconds
E in J, I in A, V in V, t in s
Direct substitution when P is not given

Finding charge; convert t to seconds

tables.title: Typical power values of common appliances

0.question: A hairdryer has a resistance of 23 Ω and draws a current of 10 A. What is its power?
0.correctFeedback: Correct! P = I²R = 10² × 23 = 100 × 23 = 2300 W = 2.3 kW. This is the right equation when you have current and resistance but not voltage.
0.wrongFeedback: Use P = I²R — square the current first (10² = 100), then multiply by resistance (100 × 23 = 2300 W). P = IV would also work if you first find V = IR = 10 × 23 = 230 V, then P = 10 × 230 = 2300 W.

1.question: A 3 kW electric heater runs for 2.5 hours. How many kWh does it use, and what does it cost at 28p per kWh?
1.correctFeedback: Perfect. Units = 3 kW × 2.5 h = 7.5 kWh. Cost = 7.5 × 28p = 210p = £2.10. Clean two-step calculation.
1.wrongFeedback: Step 1: Units (kWh) = power (kW) × time (hours) = 3 × 2.5 = 7.5 kWh. Step 2: Cost = 7.5 kWh × 28p = 210p = £2.10.

2.question: A resistor has R = 50 Ω and V = 10 V. What is the energy transferred in 2 minutes?
2.correctFeedback: Correct! First find P = V²/R = 100/50 = 2 W. Then E = Pt = 2 × 120 = 240 J. (Remember: 2 minutes = 120 seconds.)
2.wrongFeedback: This is a multi-step calculation. Step 1: P = V²/R = 10²/50 = 100/50 = 2 W. Step 2: convert 2 minutes to seconds (× 60) = 120 s. Step 3: E = Pt = 2 × 120 = 240 J.

3.question: An energy-efficient washing machine costs £150 more than a standard model but saves £50 per year on electricity bills. What is the payback time?
3.correctFeedback: Right. Payback time = extra cost ÷ annual saving = £150 ÷ £50 = 3 years. After that, the savings are pure profit.
3.wrongFeedback: Payback time = extra cost ÷ annual saving = 150 ÷ 50 = 3 years. Don't confuse the numbers — divide the upfront cost by the yearly saving.

commonMistakes: Forgetting to convert watts to kilowatts before calculating kWh — divide by 1000. A 2000 W kettle is 2 kW, not 2000 kW.
Using seconds instead of hours (or vice versa) when calculating energy — use seconds for joules (E = Pt), hours for kWh. Mixing them gives answers that are wildly wrong.
Using P = I + V or some invented equation instead of P = IV — power is a product (multiplication), not a sum.
Forgetting to square the current in P = I²R — writing P = IR instead of P = I²R gives the voltage, not the power.
Not including units in answers — exam mark schemes require the correct unit (W, J, kWh) as well as the number.
examTips: In multi-step electricity calculations, always write out every step. Examiners award marks for correct working even if your final answer is wrong — never skip steps.
The price per kWh is always given in the exam question — never memorise a fixed value. Read the question carefully and use the number provided.
When a question gives you R and I, use P = I²R directly rather than first calculating V and then P = IV — it is faster and reduces the chance of arithmetic errors.
revisionCardBullets: Power = rate of energy transfer; unit = watt (W); 1 W = 1 J/s
E = IVt (substitute P = IV into E = Pt)
1 kWh = energy from 1 kW device for 1 hour = 3.6 MJ
Payback time (years) = extra cost ÷ annual saving
Q = It (charge in coulombs = current × time in seconds)
E = QV (energy = charge × voltage; links charge to work done)
